title: Shower screen cleaner spray
subtitle: A vinegar and tea tree spray for soap scum and mould on glass shower screens
excerpt: A spray cleaner made from white vinegar, distilled water, and tea tree essential oil for removing soap scum, limescale, and surface mould from glass shower screens. Spray on, leave for 5 minutes, and wipe. Do not use on natural stone tiles.
sourceNotes: Dri-Pak white vinegar cleaning applications (dri-pak.co.uk, accessed May 2026): acetic acid dissolves calcium carbonate limescale. Carson et al., Melaleuca alternifolia antimicrobial properties (Clinical Microbiology Reviews, 2006): tea tree at 0.5-2% concentration.

recipe.batchNotes: Fill multiple spray bottles in one go; the spray keeps for 12 months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make ahead and keep under the sink. Shake before each use.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months. The vinegar remains effective indefinitely; the tea tree essential oil fades in potency after 12 months. The spray still cleans without it. Discard if the spray smells off.

glossaryTerms.definition: The white or grey film that builds up on shower screens and tiles is a mixture of calcium and magnesium salts formed when soap reacts with hard water minerals. Acetic acid in white vinegar reacts with these mineral salts and dissolves them. Limescale (calcium carbonate) dissolves by the same acid-base reaction.

Tea tree essential oil (Melaleuca alternifolia) has documented activity against surface moulds and bacteria including Aspergillus niger and some Cladosporium species commonly found in bathroom environments. At 1 to 2% concentration in a spray, it provides a low-level antimicrobial action alongside the acetic acid of the vinegar, which is itself inhibitory to many surface organisms.

content.text: from the reaction between soap and hard water minerals. The acetic acid in white vinegar dissolves these mineral deposits without scratching the glass. Tea tree essential oil adds a
action against the mould that commonly grows in shower grout and silicone seals.

Do not use this spray on natural stone tiles such as marble, travertine, or limestone: the acetic acid will etch and dull the surface. It is safe on glazed ceramic tiles, glass, stainless steel, and chrome. Tea tree essential oil is toxic to cats; keep pets out of the bathroom during and after spraying.

items.prepNote: Cleaning-grade white vinegar (5% acidity). Provides the acid to dissolve limescale and soap scum.
Dilutes the vinegar to a safe spray concentration. Do not use tap water if your area has hard water; the minerals will reduce cleaning effectiveness.
1% of total spray volume. Shake the bottle before each use to disperse the oil. Toxic to cats.

content.text: Pour the white vinegar and distilled water into the spray bottle using the funnel. Add the tea tree essential oil. Seal and shake for 30 seconds to disperse. Label with the making date.

Shake the bottle. Spray the shower screen generously, covering all soap scum and mould areas. Leave for 5 minutes; longer for heavy limescale deposits. Wipe with a microfibre cloth using light pressure. For persistent mould in silicone seals, spray and leave for 15 minutes before scrubbing with an old toothbrush. Rinse with clean water and dry the screen to prevent further mineral deposit.

Store the spray bottle under the sink or in a cupboard away from direct light. Keeps for 12 months. Shake before every use as the tea tree essential oil will float on the surface between uses. The vinegar remains fully effective after the tea tree scent fades.
